redFlags: A "remote IT job" that requires paying for training or equipment upfront
A company that sends a check for equipment
Vague job posts with no company name
"IT" roles that are actually commission-only sales
$14-$16/hr help desk unless it is short-term with a clear conversion path
Data-entry jobs claiming unusually high pay with no real interview process
Interviews conducted only through text/chat with no real company email domain
verificationSteps: Search "[Company name] + scam" or "[Company name] + reviews" — a few honest complaints are normal, but a wall of scam reports is a hard stop.
Check that the company has a real website and a LinkedIn company page with actual employees listed, not just a single job post.
Match the email domain — a legitimate recruiter emails from @company.com, not Gmail, Yahoo, or a domain that is one letter off from the real company.
Never pay for training, background checks, or equipment upfront, and never accept a check to buy your own equipment — this is one of the most common remote-job scams.
Be cautious of interviews conducted entirely over text or chat apps (especially Telegram) with no video or phone call and no real company email.
Confirm the role before giving personal information — a real employer does not need a Social Security number or bank account details before an offer.
When in doubt, apply through the official company careers page instead of a link in an unsolicited message — this avoids most scam variants entirely.
